title: Mismatched Monthly Allocation Divisors Cause Final Investor Claims to Revert Due to Truncation Deficit
description:
Root cause & mechanism
The investor onboarding logic in add_investor_to_category misaligns (1) the category-level “reservation/refill rate” used to grow the amount considered reserved for investor claims and (2) the investor-level monthly claim rate used by investor_claim_tokens. This happens because, when an investor is treated as joining mid-vesting, the function converts one month of vesting into an extra 1-month cliff (to “skip the remainder of the first month” since adding happens half-way through a cycle) by:
- setting investor.cliff_months_remaining = 1
- decrementing investor.vesting_months_remaining by 1

But it then updates category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly using total_allocation_in_base_units divided by (investor.vesting_months_remaining + 1) (i.e., the original remaining vesting-month count before the decrement), while computing investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units using total_allocation_in_base_units divided by investor.vesting_months_remaining (i.e., the reduced month count after the decrement). Both are integer (floor) divisions. Splitting the same total across two different divisors causes a systematic rounding mismatch.

This mismatch propagates into category-level accounting:
- tokens_ready_for_claim (the value intended to represent tokens that must remain in the category ATA to satisfy investor claims, and which is used as the upper limit for manual category withdrawal) is increased during category vesting updates (update_vesting_for_investor_category) using:
  category.tokens_ready_for_claim += category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly * vesting_months_claimed
- Because total_allocated_tokens_monthly is understated for late-joined investors, tokens_ready_for_claim grows too slowly relative to what those investors will later claim.

Additionally, for mid-vesting joins the code implicitly relies on “historical” vested tokens already sitting in the category ATA (from months before the investor was added) to cover part of the shortfall, but those historical balances are never reserved in tokens_ready_for_claim, breaking its intended meaning.

A further logic error broadens when the buggy “mid-vesting” branch is taken: the branch condition checks only investor.cliff_months_remaining == 0 && investor.vesting_months_remaining > 0 and does not verify that TGE/vesting has started (category.cliff_started_at != 0). Therefore, in categories configured with no cliff, investors added before TGE can be incorrectly treated as mid-vesting joiners and forced into the same synthetic-cliff + mismatched-divisor path.

Trigger conditions / assumptions
- Mid-vesting / late join path is taken when investor.cliff_months_remaining == 0 && investor.vesting_months_remaining > 0 at onboarding.
  - Common case: the category cliff has ended and the category is actively vesting.
  - In non-local-testing builds, onboarding is only allowed when the category is “up to date” (full_months_since_last_claim must be 0, else ErrorCode::CategoryLevelUnclaimed), meaning the investor can be added only when there are no unclaimed category-level months pending.
- The erroneous pre-TGE trigger occurs when:
  - category.cliff_months_remaining == 0 (no cliff category),
  - category.cliff_started_at == 0 (TGE not started),
  - category.vesting_months_remaining > 0,
  causing the same mid-vesting penalty logic to execute despite no vesting cycles having started.
- The rounding mismatch is most apparent for allocations where floor(Total / V) * V differs from floor(Total / (V-1)) * (V-1). One described sufficient condition: total_allocation % V < total_allocation % (V + 1) (where V is the reduced vesting-month count), which makes category accumulation strictly less than investor entitlement.
- SPL Token transfers are all-or-nothing on insufficient balance; the code comment “// we can afford minor integer division error” is unsafe under these mechanics.

Impact
- Under-reservation / understated liabilities: category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly understates the true per-month entitlement implied by investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units for investors routed through the synthetic-cliff path. Consequently, each transfer_category_vestings/update_vesting_for_investor_category cycle under-increments tokens_ready_for_claim.
- Withdrawals can drain investor-entitled funds: withdraw_category_tokens relies on tokens_ready_for_claim and treats (category_ata.amount - tokens_ready_for_claim) as withdrawable “excess”. Because tokens_ready_for_claim is too low (and historical balances are not reserved), an honest operator can withdraw what appears to be surplus while still satisfying on-chain checks, even though those tokens are owed to late-added investors. In fragmented open categories this can free up at least 1 extra whole SBT that should have remained reserved.
- Denial of service / frozen claims due to dust deficit:
  - If the category ATA balance becomes even slightly short, investor_claim_tokens refuses to transfer and returns early with a message (“No available tokens in the category at the moment. You can always try again.”), leaving the investor unpaid for that claim window.
  - In other flows/implementations described, attempting to withdraw the exact monthly allocation against a slightly underfunded tracked balance can underflow and revert, permanently locking the final month’s tokens for the investor.
  - Either way, a tiny rounding “dust” gap can block a much larger matured claim until someone manually tops up the category vault.
- Incorrect vesting schedule for pre-TGE investors in no-cliff categories: such investors are unfairly penalized with an unintended 1-month cliff, causing them to receive 0 tokens during the first month after TGE, and they are also routed into the same mismatched-divisor logic that can later produce an underfunding/last-claim failure.

Exploitation / reproduction notes (PoC highlights)
1) Late-join rounding deficit leading to final-claim failure (SBT_DECIMALS = 6):
- Ensure the category has passed its cliff and is actively vesting (cliff_months_remaining == 0, vesting_months_remaining > 0), and in non-local-testing ensure full_months_since_last_claim == 0 so adding is allowed.
- Add an investor with category_add_investor / add_investor_to_category using an amount that creates unfavorable rounding, e.g.:
  a) Example A (19→18 months):
     - Suppose 19 vesting months remaining.
     - Allocate 100 SBT (100,000,000 base units) such that (amount % 18) < (amount % 19).
     - Onboarding sets investor.cliff_months_remaining = 1 and reduces vesting_months_remaining to 18.
     - Category reservation increment uses 100,000,000 / 19 = 5,263,157; over 19 months this is 99,999,983.
     - Investor monthly allocation is 100,000,000 / 18 = 5,555,555; over 18 months investor expects 99,999,990.
     - After 17 successful claims, the 18th claim attempts 5,555,555 but only 5,555,548 remains allocated (deficit 7 base units), causing the claim to fail (insufficient balance / underflow revert depending on the path), effectively locking the final month (~5.5% of total allocation in that example narrative).
  b) Example B (24→23 months):
     - Start with category.vesting_months_remaining == 24 and cliff_months_remaining == 0.
     - Allocate 100 SBT (100,000,000 base units).
     - Onboarding reduces investor.vesting_months_remaining from 24 to 23 and sets investor.cliff_months_remaining = 1.
     - Category total_allocated_tokens_monthly increases by 100,000,000 / 24 = 4,166,666 (summing to 99,999,984 over 24 months).
     - Investor monthly allocation is 100,000,000 / 23 = 4,347,826 (summing to 99,999,998 over 23 months).
     - After 22 claims (95,652,172), the final claim attempts 4,347,826 but only 4,347,812 remains if funding followed the category reservation math, so the transfer fails due to insufficient balance.
2) Under-reservation enables “surplus” withdrawal then claim freeze:
- Add an investor mid-vesting as above (creating allocation/r vs allocation/(r-1) mismatch).
- Let transfer_category_vestings run over time; update_vesting_for_investor_category increases tokens_ready_for_claim by category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly * vesting_months_claimed, which is too small for the late-joined investor.
- Call withdraw_category_tokens; because tokens_ready_for_claim is understated and pre-join historical balances are not reserved, the withdraw logic can approve withdrawing tokens actually needed for future investor claims.
- Later, investor_claim_tokens computes total_tokens = investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units * vesting_months_claimed and requires category_ata.amount >= total_tokens; if slightly short, it returns without transferring anything, freezing the matured payout until a manual top-up.
3) Pre-TGE no-cliff misclassification:
- Initialize a category with cliff_months_remaining = 0, vesting_months_remaining = 24, and with TGE not started (cliff_started_at = 0).
- Add an investor before TGE.
- Because investor.cliff_months_remaining is inherited as 0 and vesting_months_remaining > 0, the “joined during vesting” branch executes even though no cycles started, giving an unintended 1-month cliff and reducing vesting to 23.
- After TGE and one month, the investor’s first claim yields 0 due to the unintended cliff month.

Code-location anchors (if present in the inputs)
- programs/master-token-program/src/instructions/investor/add.rs (also shown in programs/master-token-program/src/lib.rs via category_add_investor wrapper)
  - Function: add_investor_to_category
  - Trigger branch: if investor.cliff_months_remaining == 0 && investor.vesting_months_remaining > 0 { ... }
  - Synthetic cliff changes: investor.cliff_months_remaining = 1; investor.vesting_months_remaining -= 1;
  - Mismatched reservation divisor:
    category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly += total_allocation_in_base_units / (investor.vesting_months_remaining + 1) as u64;
  - Investor claim-rate divisor:
    investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units = total_allocation_in_base_units / investor.vesting_months_remaining as u64;
  - Related checks/errors: ErrorCode::CategoryLevelUnclaimed gate (non-local-testing), ErrorCode::VestingScheduleFinished
- programs/master-token-program/src/instructions/category/claim.rs
  - transfer_category_vestings calls update_vesting_for_investor_category across investor categories
  - update_vesting_for_investor_category:
    - require!(category.cliff_started_at != 0, ErrorCode::TgeNotHappened)
    - updates: category.tokens_ready_for_claim += category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly * vesting_months_claimed as u64
- programs/master-token-program/src/instructions/investor/claim.rs
  - investor_claim_tokens computes:
    total_tokens = investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units * vesting_months_claimed as u64
  - All-or-nothing balance check:
    if category_ata.amount < total_tokens { return Ok(()); }
  - Accounting decrement uses saturating_sub:
    category.tokens_ready_for_claim = category.tokens_ready_for_claim.saturating_sub(total_tokens)
- Implicated state/vars: InvestorCategoryData.total_allocated_tokens_monthly, InvestorCategoryData.tokens_ready_for_claim, category_ata.amount, Investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units, Investor.vesting_months_remaining, Investor.cliff_months_remaining, category.cliff_started_at, category.months_claimed, investor.last_offset_months

final_verdict.reason: While `add_investor_to_category` does use different divisors for `category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly` (uses pre-decrement months) vs `investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units` (uses post-decrement months) in the “joined during vesting” branch, this is explicitly intentional per the inline comments to account for a synthetic 1-month cliff. The finding’s core claimed impact is incorrect: `investor_claim_tokens` does not revert on insufficient balance; it simply returns `Ok(())` without transferring, so there is no deterministic “final claim revert” as described. Additionally, `withdraw_category_tokens` is capped by `category.unallocated_tokens_left` (which is reduced by the full investor allocation on onboarding), so the report does not show a realistic path where the rounding mismatch lets withdrawal drain already-allocated investor principal. The “pre-TGE no-cliff misclassification” scenario is not applicable to this codebase’s investor categories because they all have nonzero `cliff_months_remaining` before TGE, so the late-join branch cannot trigger pre-TGE.

While `add_investor_to_category` does use different divisors for `category.total_allocated_tokens_monthly` (uses pre-decrement months) vs `investor.monthly_allocation_in_base_units` (uses post-decrement months) in the “joined during vesting” branch, this is explicitly intentional per the inline comments to account for a synthetic 1-month cliff. The finding’s core claimed impact is incorrect: `investor_claim_tokens` does not revert on insufficient balance; it simply returns `Ok(())` without transferring, so there is no deterministic “final claim revert” as described. Additionally, `withdraw_category_tokens` is capped by `category.unallocated_tokens_left` (which is reduced by the full investor allocation on onboarding), so the report does not show a realistic path where the rounding mismatch lets withdrawal drain already-allocated investor principal. The “pre-TGE no-cliff misclassification” scenario is not applicable to this codebase’s investor categories because they all have nonzero `cliff_months_remaining` before TGE, so the late-join branch cannot trigger pre-TGE.

The finding’s core claimed impact is incorrect: `investor_claim_tokens` does not revert on insufficient balance; it simply returns `Ok(())` without transferring, so there is no deterministic “final claim revert” as described. Additionally, `withdraw_category_tokens` is capped by `category.unallocated_tokens_left` (which is reduced by the full investor allocation on onboarding), so the report does not show a realistic path where the rounding mismatch lets withdrawal drain already-allocated investor principal. The “pre-TGE no-cliff misclassification” scenario is not applicable to this codebase’s investor categories because they all have nonzero `cliff_months_remaining` before TGE, so the late-join branch cannot trigger pre-TGE.

invalid_rules_check.prompt:
You are the best security expert in web3 security auditing. 
    Your task is to check whether a given security finding matches any known invalid finding patterns and rules.
    
    **Instructions and Requirements**:
    1. Carefully analyze the finding and compare it against every single rule in the provided InvalidFindingRules.
    2. If a match is found, provide a concise explanation of why the finding is invalid based on the matched rule and set the verdict field to be "invalid".
    3. If no match is found, set the verdict field to "n/a" and set the reason field to "No invalid rules matched, further investigation needed".
    4. You can terminate the analysis early if you find a rule that matches the finding and hence marked the finding as invalid.
    5. Be strict: vague or speculative matches should be excluded. That is, only mark the finding as invalid if it clearly and directly falls under the rule's description. 
    6. You must check against each rule in the InvalidFindingRules list one by one, until a match is found or all rules have been checked.

    ** Output **
    Your output should be an instance of the AIRawFindingVerificationV2 class, which contains the following fields:
    - verdict:  A string indicating the verdict of the finding. At this stage, you can only set it to "invalid" if the finding matches any of the InvalidFindingRules or "n/a" if the finding does not match any of the InvalidFindingRules.
    - confidence: A string indicating the confidence level of the verdict. It can be "Strong", "Medium", or "Weak".
    - reason: A string explaining the reason for the verdict. It should be concise and clear in less than 5 sentences.
    - model: Do not set this field. Leave it empty.

    ** Input **
    Input 1. A **finding** in JSON format to be checked as delimited by <finding> tag.

    Input 2. A list of **InvalidFindingRules** in JSON format as delimited by <InvalidFindingRules> tag. 
    <InvalidFindingRules>
    [{"id": 1, "name": "no_exploitable_issue", "description": "If the finding indicates that no exploitable issue were found, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "No vulnerabilities found in the contract."}, {"id": 2, "name": "privileged_roles", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid if it requires the attacker to already possess privileged permissions (owner/admin/minter/manager/signer, etc.) AND the reported impact is fully achievable by that role through intended/explicitly authorized functionality (i.e., it is not a bypass, invariant break, or escalation beyond the documented role powers). If the finding shows that a privileged role can exceed its intended authority, bypass multisig/threshold, break safety invariants, steal assets contrary to policy, or permanently lock the system without an intended recovery path, then this rule does NOT apply.", "example": "invalid example: Owner can withdraw funds from the contract, and withdrawals are explicitly permitted by the contract design.
 n/a example: A single signatory can update metadata even though other administrative actions are protected by a multisig threshold, indicating a bypass of the intended authorization model."}, {"id": 3, "name": "first_caller_wins", "description": "If the finding describes a 'first-caller wins' scenario in functions related to initialization or similar (such as `initialize` function in many Solidity projects), it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "The first caller of the initialize function can set arbitrary values."}, {"id": 4, "name": "fee_on_transfer", "description": "If the finding is related to fee-on-transfer behaviour—i.e., transfer or transferFrom deducts a fee but still emits a single Transfer event with the original value—treat this discrepancy between events and state as non-critical and mark the finding as invalid.", "example": "Transfer event emitted with original value despite fee deduction."}, {"id": 5, "name": "requires_external_manipulation", "description": "If the exploit assumes the attacker can trick, persuade, or manipulate a privileged user to call a function, it should be marked as invalid. The exploit must be feasible without external influence.", "example": "Assuming a user is tricked into calling a transfer function with malicious input."}, {"id": 6, "name": "assumes_owner_mistake", "description": "If the finding assumes the contract owner makes a mistake (e.g., sets wrong values, uses incorrect proxies), it should be marked as invalid. But if it involves a coding mistake such as unintialized variables or roles, this rule does not apply.", "example": "Owner might accidentally set an unsafe high interest rate."}, {"id": 7, "name": "impractical_overflow_underflow", "description": "If the finding involves overflow or underflow that depends on impractically large values (e.g., approaching uint256 max or e18), and is not exploitable in realistic usage, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "Array index can overflow only if index > 2^255."}, {"id": 8, "name": "requires_privilege_escalation", "description": "If the finding assumes the attacker gains privileged permissions (such as onlyOwner, onlyMinter) through unspecified or unrealistic means, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "Assuming an attacker can trick, persuade, or manipulate the legitimate owner or another address to call a function, it should be considered invalid."}, {"id": 9, "name": "unrealistic_high_cost_attack", "description": "If the finding assumes the attacker would spend excessive resources, time, or money for a minimal or temporary impact (e.g., short-term DoS), it should be marked as invalid. Attacks should be cost-effective and realistic.", "example": "Attacker must spend 1 BTC to congest the network and temporarily disrupt service."}, {"id": 10, "name": "pure_operator_error_no_missing_validation", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid only if the described issue requires an owner/user/operator to provide incorrect configuration or parameters AND the contract already enforces all relevant on-chain validation/invariants such that correct usage prevents the issue. If the contract lacks validation (e.g., does not verify interfaces, does not bound-check parameters, accepts non-canonical inputs, or fails open on external dependency failure), then the finding is coding-related and must NOT be marked invalid under this rule.", "example": "Invalid: documentation clearly requires a specific precondition and the contract enforces it, but the finding assumes the operator ignores it. Valid: the contract does not validate that a configured external contract exposes required views, so misconfiguration can cause incorrect behavior."}, {"id": 11, "name": "incomplete_finding_description", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid if the finding description is obviously truncated or incomplete (e.g., abruptly cut off mid-sentence, unfinished bullet/section, dangling punctuation like an opening quote/parenthesis/bracket without a close, etc.). If the description appears complete (even if unclear or potentially incorrect), then this rule does NOT apply and should be marked as N/A. ", "example": {"invalid": "The attacker can drain funds by calling withdraw and", "n_a": "The attacker can drain funds by exploiting a missing access control check on withdraw(), allowing unauthorized transfers."}}]
    </InvalidFindingRules>

result.reason: No invalid rules matched, further investigation needed. The finding describes a concrete accounting/rounding mismatch and mis-branching that can under-reserve tokens and cause failed claims or unintended withdrawals, which is not merely an intended privileged action, not dependent on owner/user mistake assumptions, and not an impractical overflow/underflow scenario. The report is detailed and complete rather than truncated.
